name: Tokio
what_it_does: The de facto standard async runtime for Rust. Provides the event loop, async I/O (TCP, UDP, files), timers, task spawning, channels, and synchronization primitives for async Rust programs. Almost every production Rust web server, gRPC service, and network application uses Tokio. Axum, Tonic, Hyper, and Reqwest all run on Tokio.
use_cases: Power async Rust agent backends with a multi-threaded work-stealing scheduler for maximum I/O throughput
Build high-concurrency agent services that handle thousands of simultaneous LLM API calls without blocking threads
Implement agent task queues using Tokio channels (mpsc, broadcast, watch) for inter-task communication
Write async agent tools that fetch data from multiple APIs concurrently with tokio::join! and select!
Build TCP/UDP-based agent infrastructure (custom protocols, WebSocket servers) with Tokio's async I/O primitives
not_for: CPU-bound computation — Tokio's async model excels at I/O concurrency; use Rayon for CPU-parallel work
Rust beginners — async Rust with Tokio has a steep learning curve; understand ownership and lifetimes first
Simple single-threaded scripts — synchronous Rust or async-std may be simpler for trivial programs
best_when: You're building a production Rust service or agent that needs high-concurrency I/O — HTTP servers, gRPC services, database clients, or API integration backends.
avoid_when: Your workload is primarily CPU-bound rather than I/O-bound — use Rayon for data-parallel CPU work, not Tokio's async tasks.

auth.notes: Local async runtime library — no authentication required.

pricing.paid_tiers: None (Tokio Inc. offers commercial support separately)
pricing.notes: MIT license. Maintained by Tokio Inc. with broad community contribution. Stable, production-grade since 1.0 (2020).

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits — scales with hardware. Tokio handles millions of concurrent tasks on modern multi-core hardware.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Tokio runtime errors include task panics (JoinError), timeout errors, and I/O errors with OS error codes. The tokio-console tool provides runtime observability for debugging async task stalls.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Tokio is a runtime — idempotency depends on agent application logic. Task spawning and channel operations are well-defined.
known_agent_gotchas: Blocking operations (file I/O, CPU-intensive code) inside async tasks block the entire Tokio thread — use tokio::task::spawn_blocking() for all blocking work
async fn in traits requires the async-trait crate (pre-Rust 1.75) or the new native async-fn-in-trait feature — check your MSRV before choosing approach
Tokio's multi-threaded runtime requires Send bounds on spawned futures — non-Send types (Rc, RefCell) cannot be held across await points in spawned tasks
tokio::select! with multiple branches cancels the losing branches — futures must be cancel-safe or use tokio::pin! to preserve state across select iterations
Task starvation can occur if CPU-bound async code runs without yield points — insert tokio::task::yield_now().await periodically in long-running tasks
The #[tokio::main] macro creates a multi-threaded runtime by default — use #[tokio::main(flavor = "current_thread")] for single-threaded environments (WASM, tests)

security_score_components.security_notes: Core async runtime with no network calls. TLS integration via tokio-rustls or tokio-native-tls. Memory safety guaranteed by Rust's ownership system. No secret handling in runtime itself.
